title: Semantic Context for Tool Orchestration
author: Müller, Robert
summary: This paper establishes that Semantic Context (SC)—descriptive metadata about available tools—is essential for effective tool orchestration by LLMs. The authors contribute theoretical grounding via SC-LinUCB, a contextual bandit algorithm with proven regret bounds in dynamic action spaces, and validate this through LLM experiments showing SC improves in-context learning efficiency and robustness. They introduce FiReAct, a retrieval pipeline that scales tool selection to 10,000+ tools, demonstrating that semantic descriptions enable LLMs to navigate large tool spaces adaptively and sample-efficiently. This directly addresses a critical scaling challenge in agent systems.
content: Computer Science > Machine Learning arXiv:2507.10820 (cs) [Submitted on 14 Jul 2025] Title: Semantic Context for Tool Orchestration Authors: Robert Müller View a PDF of the paper titled Semantic Context for Tool Orchestration, by Robert M\"uller View PDF Abstract: This paper demonstrates that Semantic Context (SC), leveraging descriptive tool information, is a foundational component for robust tool orchestration. Our contributions are threefold. First, we provide a theoretical foundation using contextual bandits, introducing SC-LinUCB and proving it achieves lower regret and adapts favourably in dynamic action spaces. Second, we provide parallel empirical validation with Large Language Models, showing that SC is critical for successful in-context learning in both static (efficient learning) and non-stationary (robust adaptation) settings. Third, we propose the FiReAct pipeline, and demonstrate on a benchmark with over 10,000 tools that SC-based retrieval enables an LLM to effectively orchestrate over a large action space. These findings provide a comprehensive guide to building more sample-efficient, adaptive, and scalable orchestration agents. takeaways: SC-LinUCB achieves lower regret than prior methods and adapts well to dynamic action spaces using contextual bandits.
Semantic tool descriptions improve LLM in-context learning in both static and non-stationary settings.
FiReAct pipeline successfully orchestrates 10,000+ tools via semantic retrieval, enabling sample-efficient, scalable agent design.
Semantic context is foundational for robust, adaptive tool selection in large-scale LLM orchestration systems.

title: Configuring Agentic AI Coding Tools: An Exploratory Study
author: Galster, Matthias, Mohsenimofidi, Seyedmoein, Lulla, Jai Lal, Abubakar, Muhammad Auwal, Treude, Christoph, Baltes, Sebastian
summary: This empirical research paper analyzes configuration mechanisms for agentic AI coding tools, examining Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Gemini, and Codex. The authors studied 2,853 GitHub repositories to understand how developers configure these tools through version-controlled artifacts like Markdown and JSON files. They identify eight configuration mechanisms ranging from static context to executable and external integrations. Key findings reveal that Context Files dominate adoption and AGENTS.md is emerging as an interoperable standard, while advanced mechanisms like Skills and Subagents remain underutilized. The work provides an empirical baseline for understanding developer practices and motivates future research on how configuration strategies evolve and impact agent performance.
content: Computer Science > Software Engineering arXiv:2602.14690 (cs) [Submitted on 16 Feb 2026 ( v1 ), last revised 8 May 2026 (this version, v4)] Title: Configuring Agentic AI Coding Tools: An Exploratory Study Authors: Matthias Galster , Seyedmoein Mohsenimofidi , Jai Lal Lulla , Muhammad Auwal Abubakar , Christoph Treude , Sebastian Baltes View a PDF of the paper titled Configuring Agentic AI Coding Tools: An Exploratory Study, by Matthias Galster and 5 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: Agentic AI coding tools increasingly automate software development tasks. Developers can configure these tools through versioned repository-level artifacts such as Markdown and JSON files. We present a systematic analysis of configuration mechanisms for agentic AI coding tools, covering Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Gemini, and Codex. We identify eight configuration mechanisms spanning from static context to executable and external integrations and, in an empirical study of 2,853 GitHub repositories, examine whether and how they are adopted, with a detailed analysis of Context Files, Skills, and Subagents. First, Context Files dominate the configuration landscape and are often the sole mechanism in a repository, with AGENTS$.$md emerging as an interoperable standard across tools. Second, few repositories adopt advanced mechanisms such as Skills and Subagents. Skills predominantly rely on static instructions rather than executable scripts. Third, distinct configuration practices are forming around different tools, with Claude Code users employing the broadest range of mechanisms. These findings establish an empirical baseline for understanding how developers configure agentic tools, suggest that AGENTS$.$md serves as a natural starting point, and motivate longitudinal and experimental research on how configuration strategies evolve and affect agent performance. Comments: 10 pages, 7 figures, 3 tables, Proceedings of the 3rd ACM/IEEE International Conference on AI-powered Software (AIware 2026) Subjects: Software Engineering (cs.SE) Cite as: arXiv:2602.14690 [cs.SE] (or arXiv:2602.14690v4 [cs.SE] for this version) https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2602.14690 Focus to learn more arXiv-issued DOI via DataCite Submission history From: Sebastian Baltes [ view email ] [v1] Mon, 16 Feb 2026 12:24:28 UTC (217 KB) [v2] Fri, 20 Mar 2026 22:48:47 UTC (206 KB) [v3] Thu, 9 Apr 2026 15:25:44 UTC (201 KB) [v4] Fri, 8 May 2026 20:59:28 UTC (240 KB) Full-text links: Access Paper: View a PDF of the paper titled Configuring Agentic AI Coding Tools: An Exploratory Study, by Matthias Galster and 5 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) TeX Source view license Current browse context: cs.SE < prev | next > new | recent | 2026-02 Change to browse by: cs References & Citations NASA ADS Google Scholar Semantic Scholar export BibTeX citation Loading... takeaways: Eight configuration mechanisms identified across agentic AI tools, spanning static context to executable integrations and external connections.
Context Files dominate the configuration landscape; AGENTS.md standard emerging as preferred interoperable approach across different tools.
Skills and Subagents adopted rarely; when used, Skills rely on static instructions rather than executable scripts.
Distinct configuration practices forming around specific tools, with Claude Code users employing broadest range of mechanisms.

title: A Practical Guide To Building AI Agents
summary: This practical guide explores the architectural principles and design patterns for building production-grade AI agents. It covers essential components including language models, memory systems, tool integration, and planning mechanisms that enable agents to operate autonomously in real-world applications. The article provides concrete guidance on structuring agent systems for reliability, scalability, and maintainability—critical concerns for teams deploying AI agents beyond proof-of-concepts into production environments.
takeaways: Design agents with modular tool integration allowing flexible composition of capabilities and external integrations
Implement robust memory architectures combining short-term context and long-term knowledge bases for coherent reasoning
Use planning frameworks to decompose complex tasks into actionable steps with clear success criteria
Establish monitoring and error recovery mechanisms to handle agent failures gracefully in production systems
Separate concerns between orchestration, LLM reasoning, and tool execution for easier maintenance and debugging

title: Evaluate Agents
summary: This article discusses frameworks and methodologies for evaluating AI agents, covering key metrics and assessment approaches. It addresses how to measure agent performance across different tasks and domains, including metrics for accuracy, efficiency, and goal completion. Understanding agent evaluation is critical for developers building and deploying autonomous systems, as it ensures agents behave reliably and meet performance requirements before production use.
takeaways: Define clear metrics: accuracy, latency, cost, and task completion rates tailored to specific agent use cases.
Use benchmark datasets and standardized tests to compare agent performance objectively across different implementations.
Implement trace analysis and logging to debug agent reasoning and identify failure modes systematically.
Evaluate both individual components and end-to-end agent behavior to catch integration issues.
Consider human-in-the-loop evaluation for subjective quality assessment alongside automated metrics.

title: https://discuss.huggingface.co/t/api-orchestration-and-ai-agent-approach/175619
summary: This discussion thread explores API orchestration and AI agent approaches in the context of LLM tool use. The post examines how language models can be enhanced to call external APIs and coordinate multiple tools to accomplish complex tasks. The relevance lies in understanding practical patterns for building agentic systems that extend LLM capabilities beyond text generation, enabling autonomous task completion through structured API interactions and tool composition.
takeaways: LLMs can dynamically select and invoke APIs based on task requirements rather than relying on fixed pipelines
Tool orchestration requires defining clear schemas so agents understand available APIs, parameters, and expected outputs
Agent frameworks benefit from state management and error handling to recover from failed tool calls
Composition of multiple tools enables LLMs to solve multi-step problems autonomously

title: A New Framework for Evaluating Voice Agents (EVA)
summary: This article introduces EVA (a framework for evaluating voice agents), developed by ServiceNow-AI. The framework appears to be a technical tool designed to assess and compare voice agent performance across multiple dimensions. Voice agents are becoming increasingly prevalent in enterprise and consumer applications, making systematic evaluation critical. The EVA framework aims to provide standardized metrics and methodology for voice agent assessment, addressing a gap in reproducible evaluation practices. Technical readers should care because reliable evaluation frameworks are foundational for improving voice agent quality and enabling fair comparison between competing approaches or vendor solutions.
takeaways: EVA provides standardized metrics for systematic voice agent evaluation and comparison.
Framework developed by ServiceNow-AI with 213+ stars and active maintenance.
Addresses need for reproducible, systematic voice agent assessment methodology.
Enables fair benchmarking across different voice agent implementations.

title: Multi Agent Research System
summary: This article describes a multi-agent research system designed as a production AI agent architecture. The system appears to coordinate multiple specialized agents to conduct research tasks, likely combining different capabilities for information retrieval, analysis, synthesis, and reasoning. For technical readers building or evaluating AI systems, understanding how to architect multiple agents working in concert—handling task decomposition, inter-agent communication, and result aggregation—is critical for scaling AI applications beyond single-model chatbots.
takeaways: Multi-agent systems decompose complex research tasks into specialized, parallel workflows
Effective inter-agent coordination requires clear communication protocols and shared context
Production architectures must handle agent fallback, error recovery, and result validation
Agent specialization (retrieval, synthesis, reasoning) improves both speed and accuracy
System observability and monitoring across agents is essential for debugging and optimization

title: Writing Tools For Agents
summary: This article examines writing tools designed for AI agents, focusing on how agents can generate, edit, and refine text as part of their broader capabilities. The piece explores the architectural patterns and design considerations for integrating writing tools into agent systems, addressing challenges like prompt engineering, output quality, and iterative refinement. The work is relevant to practitioners building production AI systems who need practical guidance on composing reliable agent-based writing functionality that maintains consistency and meets user requirements.
takeaways: AI agents need specialized writing tools for text generation, editing, and refinement tasks rather than relying solely on base model outputs
Architectural patterns should separate concerns between reasoning, planning, and text composition to improve quality and debuggability
Iterative refinement and feedback loops help agents improve writing output over multiple passes or with user guidance

title: Changes
summary: This article discusses emerging design patterns for agentic systems in 2026, focusing on how autonomous agents are evolving in architecture and implementation. The piece addresses the shift from static, rule-based systems toward more dynamic, adaptive agent frameworks that can reason, plan, and act autonomously. Technical practitioners building AI systems will benefit from understanding these evolving patterns to design more capable and robust autonomous systems.
takeaways: Agentic systems are shifting from rigid rule-based architectures to adaptive, reasoning-capable frameworks
Agent design patterns emphasize modularity and composability for complex multi-step tasks
Autonomous decision-making requires robust error handling and feedback loops for reliability

title: Paper page - Natural-Language Agent Harnesses
summary: This appears to be a paper metadata or index page for an LLM agent harness survey, listing repository information and engagement metrics. The source suggests content around designing effective harnesses or frameworks for language model-based agents—systems that coordinate LLM behavior with external tools and memory. For technical readers building production AI agents, harness design is critical for reliability, composability, and control. However, the provided content lacks substantive detail about specific methodologies, findings, or architectural patterns that would constitute actionable guidance.
takeaways: LLM agent harness design is an active research area with growing community interest
Effective harnesses coordinate models with external tools and state management
Repository-based surveys help practitioners benchmark and compare agent frameworks
